lines.text: Section ten. RNA as a regulator, and the riboswitches that synthetic biology has begun to put to work.
It is tempting to think of RNA as just the passive messenger — a tape that carries the message from DNA to ribosome and then gets destroyed.
For decades that is roughly how molecular biology thought about it.
We now know that view was incomplete, and in some ways quite wrong.
RNA does many things in the cell.
It does carry messages, yes.
But it also performs catalysis, and it actively regulates gene expression.
Its versatility is now believed to reflect a very ancient role.

There is a widely supported hypothesis, called the RNA world hypothesis, which proposes that very early in the history of life — perhaps before DNA existed at all — RNA served both as the storage molecule for genetic information and as the catalytic molecule that carried out cellular reactions.
In the RNA world, RNA was both database and machine.
DNA arose later as a more chemically stable carrier — better suited to long-term storage and to building genomes large enough for complex organisms.
Proteins arose to take over catalysis at scale, far more efficient and diverse than RNA at building specialised enzymes.
But RNA never quite handed everything over.
Some of the oldest and most central processes in modern cells are still done with RNA, or with RNA-protein hybrids.

The ribosome itself, which we have been talking about, is a perfect example: it is a massive ribonucleoprotein complex, but the catalytic centre that actually forms the peptide bond is made of RNA, not protein.
The ribosome is, at its heart, a ribozyme — an RNA enzyme.
Transfer RNA, similarly, is RNA doing a sophisticated structural and recognition job that proteins do not.
Even outside the ribosome, RNA does catalysis: some RNAs splice themselves; some cut other RNAs; we call these catalytic RNAs ribozymes generally.
But the discovery that has shaped molecular biology most over the last twenty-five years is RNA's role in regulating gene expression through small RNAs.
In eukaryotes, the discovery of small interfering RNA — siRNA — and the related microRNA system has opened up an entirely new layer of gene control.
These short RNAs bind to specific mRNAs by base pairing, recruit protein machinery, and either degrade the mRNA or block its translation.
Beyond the basic biology, siRNA has become a workhorse tool: in the lab, you can design a short RNA to silence essentially any gene you want, with high specificity.

Prokaryotes do not have the same elaborate machinery as eukaryotes do, but they also use small RNAs to regulate gene expression, and synthetic biologists have built versions of small-RNA control in bacteria.
An influential paper by Travis Bayer and Christina Smolke in 2005 showed that you can rationally engineer small RNAs that bind to specific mRNAs and modulate translation in predictable ways.
An attractive feature of RNA-based regulation is its speed.
RNA molecules are made quickly and degraded quickly — their typical half-lives are short.

That means RNA-based regulation can turn on and off much faster than protein-based regulation, where you have to first transcribe the regulator, then translate it, then have the regulator find its target.
Proteins are also costly: they take more energy to build, last longer than you might want, and clutter up the cell with leftover regulator after the moment of decision has passed.
RNA regulators sidestep some of that cost.
And then there is a particular class of RNA regulators that is especially elegant: riboswitches.

A riboswitch is a stretch of RNA, usually sitting in the 5-prime untranslated region of an mRNA, that can fold into two different shapes — and that physically changes shape when a small metabolite molecule binds to it.
Imagine a length of RNA sitting just upstream of an RBS.
When no small molecule is present, the RNA adopts one structure that leaves the RBS exposed, ribosome happy, translation proceeds.

When the relevant small molecule is around — say, an amino acid that the cell happens to be metabolising — the RNA binds the small molecule and refolds into a different structure that buries the RBS in a hairpin, ribosome cannot dock, translation stops.
The riboswitch has, in effect, sensed the concentration of a metabolite directly, without needing a protein sensor at all.
In nature, riboswitches are often used as feedback regulators on metabolic pathways: when the metabolic product accumulates, the riboswitch shuts off translation of the enzymes that produce it.
It is a beautiful, low-cost feedback control built entirely out of RNA and a small molecule.
Tucker and Breaker described many natural riboswitches in a 2005 review that the textbook recommends.
And synthetic biologists have engineered riboswitches into circuits — Callura and colleagues in 2010 showed riboswitches incorporated into programmable bacterial control modules.
If you treat them as engineered parts, riboswitches give you a way to make protein expression directly conditional on chemical signals, with no extra protein machinery required.
When you put all of this together — the central dogma, the levels of control, the role of RNA — what you should see is a layered architecture.
Information flows from DNA to RNA to protein.
But at every level, the cell has knobs.
It can tune transcription via promoters, sigma factors, activators, and repressors.
It can tune translation via the RBS and via secondary structure in mRNA.
It can tune everything again with regulatory RNAs and riboswitches.
Every one of those knobs is, in principle, available to a synthetic biologist as a design surface.
The rest of this book is, in a real sense, a tour of how to design at those surfaces.
That is the end of chapter one.
